speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number 12, an interview with Mr. Stefan Stok. At the end of the last tape you were telling me about your graduation-- your graduation to be an architect. And about your father-- that they had a certificate to become an Israeli lawyer, but then you moved to Brazil with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you were-- you stayed-- you yourself, did you stay in Israel or for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I stayed, yes, because they left-- is it on now? Yeah. Because I stayed in Israel when they left, because they left in 1958 and I was still in the middle of my studies. And I didn't feel that I want to interrupt my studies. So I did go through all my studies to the end, which-- because I left some exams to take them later. It went on until 1960. And then I went and I traveled around Europe quite a bit. I traveled around Europe in 1959, and that was in the holidays from-- but long holidays from [INAUDIBLE] and then again in 1960. And I went back to Poland even in 1960 on a kind of a student exchange scheme for about 2 months. And then I wanted to go back to Brazil to see my parents, but I decided that first, I want to stay-- work a bit in Europe and see how these things were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started-- I came to Europe again in 1960 and found a job in Paris and stayed for some months in Paris. And then I met my future wife, who lived near Frankfurt in Germany. And she suggested to me that I could find a place to work in Frankfurt. So I then moved from Paris to Frankfurt and stayed there for about a year, year and half almost, a bit less, and worked in an architect's office there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went back finally in '63-- early in beginning of '63 to Brazil and stayed about a year in Brazil and traveled throughout South America and came back once I decided South America was-- didn't suit me completely. I wanted to go back to Europe. So I came back to Europe and I met my girlfriend again, and we got married in 1964. And then there was a question-- got married in Frankfurt-- and there was a question where to live. And I was-- I didn't want to go immediately back to Israel, because I wanted to stay a bit in Europe and-- and work and live here. And I-- there were-- I thought about going to England or maybe going to Italy, but I went first to London. And in '64-- and found a work place as an architect in architect's office here in London. And came with my wife in May to-- in May 1964 to London. And we thought then that it was going only to be for a limited time, but it turned otherwise, and we have been living in London ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And-- and we have three children, two girls who are now grown up, and a boy of 16. And have already 3 grandchildren from the oldest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you still working now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not working.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? You're retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So OK. So we're coming now to the end of this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't think. I think I've been speaking for such a long time. Yeah, I think we are going to make a break, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is there any-- any message that come out of your story of your life that you would like to express?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think that there is a particular message of any kind. I think it is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those are things which I'm-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's, of course, a very traumatic period in ones life, but perhaps it is better if one can still talk about it now before those things get totally forgotten. And I must say that it is-- it is-- in a way, it was probably it would have been difficult to make this kind of interview in earlier years. Because for many years-- you are probably aware for many years after the war for one reason or another, one didn't want to talk about those things. One didn't want to talk about the war. It was-- its' a pity in a way, because probably very many of the testimonies that were there very important have never been said and have never been written. And people who had probably much more important things to say never managed to say them. But there as well for many years a kind of a conspiracy of silence that one didn't-- people who survived the war in Europe, in Poland, even in a country like Israel felt that it was something that one had to keep strictly private and-- and not to express and-- and-- and not to say openly. And now of course, the things have come to the other kind of-- of-- other kind of power that now practically many people who are still around are finally finding they have to say those things before it is too late. So but, of course, things have-- certain things have been forgotten, which it's very difficult to bring back.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you for this interview, and I wish you all the best for the years to come. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stefan [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Stefan, Who was in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photograph of my mother and me in one of the very few photographs from before the war on the seaside at Sopot, not far from Gdansk. In probably 1936 or 1937. Anyway, before the war. That's, I think, one of the very few ones that remains.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when should I start speaking?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now. Who was in this picture? You can tell us now.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my photograph, Stefan Stok, in-- that's the only photograph I had from the ghetto, which was done in the-- by the photographer Doritz, who was very well known in Warsaw. And that was in 1942, probably in Spring 1942. Maybe in April or March or April.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old you were at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was eight-- eight-- eight and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, what's in this picture? Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's Marta Koper, the woman who kept us for the last year and a half of the war in Warsaw, which I talked about earlier. And we really owe our-- we really owe our lives thanks to-- to her and-- and thanks to her devotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is Stefan Koper, her husband of Marta in whose house we stayed in Warsaw from May '43 to September '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this photograph? Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my photograph of my father, Marek Stok.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not 100% sure. It must have been after the war, but I think it's in Poland. I think it's after the war, but I'm not quite certain when.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold on. I just want to see something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is in this picture now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother, Zofia Stok. I think the photograph is after the war in-- probably early after the war-- and judging by the hairstyle in late '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's me, Stefan Stok, after arriving in Lublin pretty immediately after deliberation, probably in October 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were-- what age you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lublin, I was 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Stefan, what's this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the kennkarte. That's the false identity card in Warsaw which both my parents had, which is obviously in the name which he had during-- in the Aryan part of Warsaw. In other words, it's the-- it's the false name which he was-- which he had to assume when he got the kennkarte. And that was the identity document under the occupation. His name here is [INAUDIBLE], which is-- which I don't think he used that kennkarte very much at all, if-- because he was most of the time-- my father was most of the time in hiding. So the kennkarte was there, but I don't think he had even an opportunity of using it more than maybe-- maybe once, maybe not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. Stefan, what's this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's social security card, which was issued for my father in Warsaw in the ghetto, which in May, 1941. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- Who stamped it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why, it is stamped by the Polish or by the Social Security Office.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here it says in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. But why did he arrange it for himself [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, again, kennkarte, the same kind of false identity card issued on the Aryan side of Warsaw to my mother. And it-- and her name is here Maria [INAUDIBLE] which was her name on the Aryan side. And in 1943, I think. Everything else, except for her photograph, all the rest of-- all the other things are fictitious. In other words, the names and the date of birth and everything is fictitious, but the photograph is--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the fingerprint for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the fingerprints, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Signed by who? The Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Signed-- no, it is a German office. It says-- I can't read it at the moment, but it's-- it's probably the Warsaw-- I'm not quite sure [INAUDIBLE]. Here it is municipality obviously. And here, you see when a-- where she was-- you had to register in every place where you lived. And here she even registered outside Warsaw where she lived in village after Warsaw uprising. She lived in a in a village outside Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. So that's [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well there were more addresses even then on the card. Yes. And that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that's more like it. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I think that's the municipality of Warsaw there. [SPEAKING POLISH], which was the name of--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] [SPEAKING GERMAN], I think in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we see here, Stefan?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just to illustrate something in my story. It's a typical courtyard in the-- in typical house in Warsaw ghetto. It comes from a book called by Emmanuel Ringelblum, who-- who had wrote a kind of an-- had a kind of archive of Warsaw ghetto, which was dug-- which was dug [INAUDIBLE] hidden during the ghetto and found after the war. And the photograph comes from there as well. But that is, of course, a house that I don't know, but it's similar to the house where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who is in this picture, Stefan?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife and I, Ute Stok and Stefan Stok. And that's quite a long time ago. That's-- as you can see, I have-- my appearance has changed and all our appearances have-- appearances have changed slightly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when it was taken about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- about 1980. 1979 or 1980.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell me, who is in this lovely picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's two of my children and I last year in-- somewhere in Morocco. That's my daughter, Natalie, and my son, Marek. And myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. And who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my older daughter, Tatiana, with her daughter, Maia. But she has got actually two other children. She has got another boy, another girl who is a baby, and another boy who is now six years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to see them, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then we have that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and last picture. Who is in this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's Marek, again-- my son Marek and my grandson Joshua. And that's about two years ago, so Joshua here is about four, and Marek is about 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I can't even see them, so I don't know who is where.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK. Stefan, this is most of your family. Could you present them? At your back is your wife--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's most part of my family. [INAUDIBLE] and there behind is my wife, Ute, and my-- and next to me is my daughter, Tatiana. And behind her is my son, Marek. Marek is 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of the grandchildren, children of Tatiana are here as well. That's Joshua who is six-- and a bit more then 6, actually. And Maia who is three and a half, and Ania who is about eight months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven months. Yes, so-- and there is still one daughter, Natalie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Natalie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- she-- she's 30.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is in the middle of the children. Younger than Tatiana, but older than Marek. But she is not here at the moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Our cat as well. So-- OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Yeah. Did you-- heard pieces-- I guess you heard pieces of the life story of your father and your husband. Do you have anything to say? Anything at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joshua [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: About your life story, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm just saying I don't-- i mean, he told me quite a lot of it, so-- especially when I was-- he used to tell me about it when I was younger [INAUDIBLE]. So I know quite a lot about it. I mean, but I always thought it was quite amazing, you know. I'm not quite sure-- I'm not quite sure-- he always over dramatized it a bit. I'm not-- I'm just trying to find the right words. He always over dramatized a bit, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's a point for a 16 year old, I should think. For me, as a daughter-- I don't know. I always-- I mean, it was a history of-- of our background, and-- I don't know. From the stories, I mean, it was a total disaster, but one can only feel pride that one belonged and that my father-- well, not pride that he went through those experiences, but it was an education. Good that this video is being done so that, you know, it's-- it's a history, because it is such an important history.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people have to-- I think it would be horrible if people did-- something like this was repeated. So it is probably a good thing that this kind of thing gets put down on tape, generally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, and I have to say that I knew about my husband's childhood and [INAUDIBLE] stories since-- since we were friends before we married. But everything he said sank very deeply in my heart and that I carried it right through our marriage together with him. And that I'm very grateful that his family accepted me so kindly, and that we had, you know, so far-- also all relatives abroad are friendly and good and kind relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much, and I wish you all the best and all the happiness there could be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
